# SQL MCQ Bank - PG Program in Data Analytics & Gen AI

## Quick Outline

Topics are taken from [prompt.txt](../prompt.txt). Topic links jump to the matching difficulty section.

- [Definition-Based Concept MCQs](#definition-concept-mcqs)
- [Beginner](#beginner-section) - [Core MCQs](#beginner-core) / [Scenario-Based Questions](#beginner-scenarios)
  Topics: [Database, table, row, column fundamentals](#beginner-section); [SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY](#beginner-section); [Basic data types](#beginner-section); [INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE](#beginner-section); [Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX](#beginner-section); [GROUP BY, HAVING](#beginner-section); [Logical/comparison operators](#beginner-section)
- [Intermediate](#intermediate-section) - [Core MCQs](#intermediate-core) / [Scenario-Based Questions](#intermediate-scenarios)
  Topics: [JOINS: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS](#intermediate-section); [Subqueries](#intermediate-section); [UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT](#intermediate-section); [String and date functions](#intermediate-section); [CASE statements](#intermediate-section); [Views](#intermediate-section); [Indexes (basics)](#intermediate-section); [Constraints: PK, FK, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, CHECK](#intermediate-section); [Normalization (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)](#intermediate-section)
- [Advanced](#advanced-section) - [Core MCQs](#advanced-core) / [Scenario-Based Questions](#advanced-scenarios)
  Topics: [Window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, PARTITION BY)](#advanced-section); [CTEs and recursive CTEs](#advanced-section); [Query optimization & execution plans](#advanced-section); [Stored procedures, triggers, user-defined functions](#advanced-section); [Transactions, ACID properties, isolation levels](#advanced-section); [Indexing strategies (clustered/non-clustered)](#advanced-section); [Database design & ER diagrams](#advanced-section); [Performance tuning for large datasets](#advanced-section); [Real-life: duplicate customer entries](#advanced-section); [optimizing production dashboard queries](#advanced-section); [online store schema design](#advanced-section)

<a id="definition-concept-mcqs"></a>
## Definition-Based Concept MCQs

#### Q1. Which definition best describes database? [Concept]

- **A.** An organised collection of data that can be stored, queried, and managed.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** An organised collection of data that can be stored, queried, and managed. This definition helps analysts use database correctly in practical work.

#### Q2. Which definition best describes table? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A structure made of rows and columns that stores related data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A structure made of rows and columns that stores related data. This definition helps analysts use table correctly in practical work.

#### Q3. Which definition best describes row? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A single record in a table.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A single record in a table. This definition helps analysts use row correctly in practical work.

#### Q4. Which definition best describes column? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A named field that stores one type of attribute across rows.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** A named field that stores one type of attribute across rows. This definition helps analysts use column correctly in practical work.

#### Q5. Which definition best describes primary key? [Concept]

- **A.** A column or set of columns that uniquely identifies each row.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A column or set of columns that uniquely identifies each row. This definition helps analysts use primary key correctly in practical work.

#### Q6. Which definition best describes foreign key? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A field that links rows in one table to rows in another table.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A field that links rows in one table to rows in another table. This definition helps analysts use foreign key correctly in practical work.

#### Q7. Which definition best describes cardinality? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** The relationship pattern between entities or rows, such as one-to-one or one-to-many.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** The relationship pattern between entities or rows, such as one-to-one or one-to-many. This definition helps analysts use cardinality correctly in practical work.

#### Q8. Which definition best describes normalization? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** Organising data to reduce duplication and improve integrity.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** Organising data to reduce duplication and improve integrity. This definition helps analysts use normalization correctly in practical work.

#### Q9. Which definition best describes join? [Concept]

- **A.** A SQL operation that combines rows from related tables.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A SQL operation that combines rows from related tables. This definition helps analysts use join correctly in practical work.

#### Q10. Which definition best describes inner join? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A join that returns only matching rows from both tables.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A join that returns only matching rows from both tables. This definition helps analysts use inner join correctly in practical work.

[Back to Quick Outline](#quick-outline)

#### Q11. Which definition best describes left join? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A join that returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A join that returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. This definition helps analysts use left join correctly in practical work.

#### Q12. Which definition best describes full join? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A join that returns matching and non-matching rows from both tables.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** A join that returns matching and non-matching rows from both tables. This definition helps analysts use full join correctly in practical work.

#### Q13. Which definition best describes cross join? [Concept]

- **A.** A join that returns every combination of rows from two tables.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A join that returns every combination of rows from two tables. This definition helps analysts use cross join correctly in practical work.

#### Q14. Which definition best describes subquery? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A query nested inside another query.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A query nested inside another query. This definition helps analysts use subquery correctly in practical work.

#### Q15. Which definition best describes common table expression? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A named temporary result set defined within a query.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A named temporary result set defined within a query. This definition helps analysts use common table expression correctly in practical work.

#### Q16. Which definition best describes recursive CTE? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A CTE that references itself to process hierarchical or repeated logic.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** A CTE that references itself to process hierarchical or repeated logic. This definition helps analysts use recursive CTE correctly in practical work.

#### Q17. Which definition best describes aggregate function? [Concept]

- **A.** A function that summarises multiple rows into one result.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A function that summarises multiple rows into one result. This definition helps analysts use aggregate function correctly in practical work.

#### Q18. Which definition best describes GROUP BY? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A clause that groups rows so aggregate calculations can be applied.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A clause that groups rows so aggregate calculations can be applied. This definition helps analysts use GROUP BY correctly in practical work.

#### Q19. Which definition best describes HAVING? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A clause that filters grouped results after aggregation.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A clause that filters grouped results after aggregation. This definition helps analysts use HAVING correctly in practical work.

#### Q20. Which definition best describes WHERE? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A clause that filters rows before grouping or aggregation.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** A clause that filters rows before grouping or aggregation. This definition helps analysts use WHERE correctly in practical work.

#### Q21. Which definition best describes CASE statement? [Concept]

- **A.** Conditional SQL logic that returns values based on rules.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** Conditional SQL logic that returns values based on rules. This definition helps analysts use CASE statement correctly in practical work.

#### Q22. Which definition best describes view? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A saved query that behaves like a virtual table.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A saved query that behaves like a virtual table. This definition helps analysts use view correctly in practical work.

#### Q23. Which definition best describes index? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A database structure that can speed up data retrieval.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A database structure that can speed up data retrieval. This definition helps analysts use index correctly in practical work.

#### Q24. Which definition best describes clustered index? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** An index that defines the physical order of table data.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** An index that defines the physical order of table data. This definition helps analysts use clustered index correctly in practical work.

#### Q25. Which definition best describes non-clustered index? [Concept]

- **A.** A separate index structure that points to table rows.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A separate index structure that points to table rows. This definition helps analysts use non-clustered index correctly in practical work.

#### Q26. Which definition best describes constraint? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A database rule that protects data validity.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A database rule that protects data validity. This definition helps analysts use constraint correctly in practical work.

#### Q27. Which definition best describes unique constraint? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A rule that prevents duplicate values in selected columns.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A rule that prevents duplicate values in selected columns. This definition helps analysts use unique constraint correctly in practical work.

#### Q28. Which definition best describes NOT NULL constraint? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A rule that requires a column to contain a value.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** A rule that requires a column to contain a value. This definition helps analysts use NOT NULL constraint correctly in practical work.

#### Q29. Which definition best describes CHECK constraint? [Concept]

- **A.** A rule that limits values allowed in a column.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A rule that limits values allowed in a column. This definition helps analysts use CHECK constraint correctly in practical work.

#### Q30. Which definition best describes transaction? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A unit of database work that should complete fully or roll back.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A unit of database work that should complete fully or roll back. This definition helps analysts use transaction correctly in practical work.

#### Q31. Which definition best describes ACID? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A set of transaction properties: atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A set of transaction properties: atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability. This definition helps analysts use ACID correctly in practical work.

#### Q32. Which definition best describes isolation level? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A setting that controls how transactions interact with concurrent changes.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** A setting that controls how transactions interact with concurrent changes. This definition helps analysts use isolation level correctly in practical work.

#### Q33. Which definition best describes stored procedure? [Concept]

- **A.** A saved set of SQL statements that can be executed repeatedly.

- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** A saved set of SQL statements that can be executed repeatedly. This definition helps analysts use stored procedure correctly in practical work.

#### Q34. Which definition best describes trigger? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** SQL logic that runs automatically when a defined database event occurs.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** SQL logic that runs automatically when a defined database event occurs. This definition helps analysts use trigger correctly in practical work.

#### Q35. Which definition best describes user-defined function? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A reusable SQL routine that returns a value or table.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A reusable SQL routine that returns a value or table. This definition helps analysts use user-defined function correctly in practical work.

#### Q36. Which definition best describes execution plan? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** The database engine strategy for running a query.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** The database engine strategy for running a query. This definition helps analysts use execution plan correctly in practical work.

#### Q37. Which definition best describes query optimization? [Concept]

- **A.** Improving a query so it returns correct results more efficiently.
- **B.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** Improving a query so it returns correct results more efficiently. This definition helps analysts use query optimization correctly in practical work.

#### Q38. Which definition best describes window function? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A function that calculates across related rows without collapsing them.
- **C.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** A function that calculates across related rows without collapsing them. This definition helps analysts use window function correctly in practical work.

#### Q39. Which definition best describes partition? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.
- **C.** A subset of rows used by a window function or storage design.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A subset of rows used by a window function or storage design. This definition helps analysts use partition correctly in practical work.

#### Q40. Which definition best describes ROW_NUMBER? [Concept]

- **A.** A visual chart setting unrelated to stored data.
- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.

**Explanation:** A window function that assigns a unique sequence number to rows. This definition helps analysts use ROW_NUMBER correctly in practical work.

#### Q43. Which definition best describes LEAD? [Concept]

- **C.** A window function that reads a value from a following row.
- **D.** A file export format used only for reporting.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** A window function that reads a value from a following row. This definition helps analysts use LEAD correctly in practical work.

- **C.** A file export format used only for reporting.
- **D.** A logical structure for organising database objects.

**Correct answer:** D

#### Q46. Which definition best describes data type? [Concept]

**Correct answer:** B

**Correct answer:** C

#### Q48. Which definition best describes referential integrity? [Concept]

- **B.** A password option used only to open the database client.

- **D.** The consistency of relationships between linked tables.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** The consistency of relationships between linked tables. This definition helps analysts use referential integrity correctly in practical work.

#### Q49. Which definition best describes deduplication? [Concept]

**Explanation:** The process of identifying and resolving duplicate records. This definition helps analysts use deduplication correctly in practical work.

<a id="beginner-section"></a>
## Beginner

<a id="beginner-core"></a>
### Core MCQs (Concept + Tool-Usage)

#### Q1. Which statement best explains the role of Database, table, row, and column fundamentals in SQL for UK data analytics work? [Concept]

- **A.** It improves reliability, interpretability, and auditability before decisions are made.
- **B.** It is mainly a visual styling choice with no effect on analytical quality.
- **C.** It guarantees that all source data is correct even when no validation is performed.
- **D.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** Database, table, row, and column fundamentals matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q2. Which practical step best demonstrates correct use of Database, table, row, and column fundamentals in a query, view, stored procedure, or schema? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Assume the default output is correct and skip all checks against the source.
- **B.** Use the tool feature deliberately, check a small sample, and document any assumption in the query, view, stored procedure, or schema.
- **C.** Change every field to text to avoid thinking about data types or aggregation.
- **D.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** Correct use of Database, table, row, and column fundamentals means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

#### Q3. What is the strongest reason to understand SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY before presenting analysis? [Concept]

- **A.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.
- **B.** It should be used only after the report has already been published.
- **C.** It reduces ambiguity in definitions, calculations, and assumptions.
- **D.** It is useful only for personal practice files and not for team analytics work.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q4. Which workflow is the best way to use SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY in production-style analysis? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.
- **B.** Build the answer manually and avoid leaving a repeatable calculation path.
- **C.** Hide errors in the final output without checking why they occurred.
- **D.** Start with the business requirement, use the correct feature, and test the output before sharing it.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** Correct use of SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

#### Q5. How should Basic data types be treated? [Concept]

- **A.** It supports repeatable analysis rather than one-off manual judgement.
- **B.** It is useful only for personal practice files and not for team analytics work.
- **C.** It replaces the need to understand the business question.
- **D.** It is relevant only when the dataset contains fewer than one hundred rows.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** Basic data types matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q6. How should Basic data types be used so the result can be checked and repeated? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Hide errors in the final output without checking why they occurred.
- **B.** Create the output, inspect edge cases, and confirm that the result answers the stakeholder question.
- **C.** Prioritise cosmetic formatting before confirming the metric definition.
- **D.** Share the output immediately and wait for stakeholders to find any issues.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** Correct use of Basic data types means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

#### Q7. Which statement best connects INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE to business decision-making? [Concept]

- **A.** It is relevant only when the dataset contains fewer than one hundred rows.
- **B.** It means the analyst no longer needs to document assumptions.
- **C.** It improves reliability, interpretability, and auditability before decisions are made.
- **D.** It is mainly a visual styling choice with no effect on analytical quality.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q8. What is the best operational habit when working with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Share the output immediately and wait for stakeholders to find any issues.
- **B.** Paste static values or screenshots so the result cannot be refreshed or audited.
- **C.** Assume the default output is correct and skip all checks against the source.
- **D.** Use the tool feature deliberately, check a small sample, and document any assumption in the query, view, stored procedure, or schema.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** Correct use of INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

#### Q9. Which statement best explains the role of Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX in SQL for UK data analytics work? [Concept]

- **A.** It reduces ambiguity in definitions, calculations, and assumptions.
- **B.** It is mainly a visual styling choice with no effect on analytical quality.
- **C.** It guarantees that all source data is correct even when no validation is performed.
- **D.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.

- **B.** It is mainly a visual styling choice with no effect on analytical quality.
- **C.** It guarantees that all source data is correct even when no validation is performed.
- **D.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q106. Which practical step best demonstrates correct use of INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE in a query, view, stored procedure, or schema? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Assume the default output is correct and skip all checks against the source.
- **B.** Start with the business requirement, use the correct feature, and test the output before sharing it.
- **C.** Change every field to text to avoid thinking about data types or aggregation.
- **D.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** Correct use of INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

- **A.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.
- **B.** Build the answer manually and avoid leaving a repeatable calculation path.
- **C.** Hide errors in the final output without checking why they occurred.
- **D.** Create the output, inspect edge cases, and confirm that the result answers the stakeholder question.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** Correct use of Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

- **D.** It is relevant only when the dataset contains fewer than one hundred rows.

- **A.** Hide errors in the final output without checking why they occurred.
- **B.** Use the tool feature deliberately, check a small sample, and document any assumption in the query, view, stored procedure, or schema.
- **C.** Prioritise cosmetic formatting before confirming the metric definition.
- **D.** Share the output immediately and wait for stakeholders to find any issues.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** Correct use of GROUP BY and HAVING means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

<a id="beginner-scenarios"></a>
### Scenario-Based Questions - Beginner

#### Q1. You are working for a UK retail chain. A manager asks you to explain a small week-on-week KPI movement. The work involves Database, table, row, and column fundamentals in SQL. What should you do first? [Scenario]

- **A.** Delete outliers or missing values before checking what they represent.
- **B.** Clarify the metric, inspect the relevant fields, apply Database, table, row, and column fundamentals in a controlled query, view, stored procedure, or schema, and validate the result before sharing.
- **C.** Change the visual styling and avoid challenging the metric definition.
- **D.** Use a manual workaround that cannot be repeated next month.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of Database, table, row, and column fundamentals, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q2. A stakeholder at a London fintech team needs a quick but reliable answer to prepare a clean summary for a team meeting. The key skill is SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY. What is the best next step? [Scenario]

- **A.** Change the visual styling and avoid challenging the metric definition.
- **B.** Use a manual workaround that cannot be repeated next month.
- **C.** Check the business question first, use SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY on the right data, and compare the output with a simple trusted total.
- **D.** Bypass governance or access controls because the deadline is tight.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q3. You receive a small data extract from an NHS reporting team and need to check why two simple totals do not match. How should you use Basic data types? [Scenario]

- **A.** Use a manual workaround that cannot be repeated next month.
- **B.** Bypass governance or access controls because the deadline is tight.
- **C.** Optimise the final screen before checking source data grain or joins.
- **D.** Preserve the raw data, create a repeatable working view, and use Basic data types only after checking basic quality issues.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of Basic data types, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q4. A non-technical colleague at a regional transport operator is unsure whether the output is trustworthy while you turn a raw export into a readable analyst note. Which approach is best? [Scenario]

- **A.** Ask what decision the output supports, apply INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and explain any assumption in plain language.
- **B.** Bypass governance or access controls because the deadline is tight.
- **C.** Optimise the final screen before checking source data grain or joins.
- **D.** Assume another team has already validated the extract and skip reconciliation.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q5. You are working for a local council analytics team. A manager asks you to spot the main category driving a change in performance. The work involves Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX in SQL. What should you do first? [Scenario]

- **A.** Optimise the final screen before checking source data grain or joins.
- **B.** Clarify the metric, inspect the relevant fields, apply Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX in a controlled query, view, stored procedure, or schema, and validate the result before sharing.
- **C.** Assume another team has already validated the extract and skip reconciliation.
- **D.** Hide caveats so the final answer looks more confident.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q6. A stakeholder at a university planning office needs a quick but reliable answer to produce a basic view for a non-technical manager. The key skill is GROUP BY and HAVING. What is the best next step? [Scenario]

- **A.** Assume another team has already validated the extract and skip reconciliation.
- **B.** Hide caveats so the final answer looks more confident.
- **C.** Check the business question first, use GROUP BY and HAVING on the right data, and compare the output with a simple trusted total.
- **D.** Send the first result immediately so the stakeholder can decide whether it feels right.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of GROUP BY and HAVING, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q7. You receive a small data extract from an energy supplier and need to explain a small week-on-week KPI movement. How should you use Logical and comparison operators? [Scenario]

- **B.** Send the first result immediately so the stakeholder can decide whether it feels right.

<a id="intermediate-section"></a>
## Intermediate

<a id="intermediate-core"></a>
### Core MCQs (Concept + Tool-Usage)

#### Q1. Which statement best explains the role of Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS in SQL for UK data analytics work? [Concept]

- **A.** It improves reliability, interpretability, and auditability before decisions are made.
- **B.** It is mainly a visual styling choice with no effect on analytical quality.
- **C.** It guarantees that all source data is correct even when no validation is performed.
- **D.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q2. Which practical step best demonstrates correct use of Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS in a query, view, stored procedure, or schema? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Assume the default output is correct and skip all checks against the source.
- **B.** Use the tool feature deliberately, check a small sample, and document any assumption in the query, view, stored procedure, or schema.
- **C.** Change every field to text to avoid thinking about data types or aggregation.
- **D.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** Correct use of Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

#### Q3. What is the strongest reason to understand Subqueries before presenting analysis? [Concept]

- **A.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.
- **B.** It should be used only after the report has already been published.
- **C.** It reduces ambiguity in definitions, calculations, and assumptions.
- **D.** It is useful only for personal practice files and not for team analytics work.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** Subqueries matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q4. Which workflow is the best way to use Subqueries in production-style analysis? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.
- **B.** Build the answer manually and avoid leaving a repeatable calculation path.
- **C.** Hide errors in the final output without checking why they occurred.
- **D.** Start with the business requirement, use the correct feature, and test the output before sharing it.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** Correct use of Subqueries means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.

#### Q5. How should UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT be treated? [Concept]

- **A.** It supports repeatable analysis rather than one-off manual judgement.
- **B.** It is useful only for personal practice files and not for team analytics work.
- **C.** It replaces the need to understand the business question.
- **D.** It is relevant only when the dataset contains fewer than one hundred rows.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q6. How should UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT be used so the result can be checked and repeated? [Tool-Usage]

- **A.** Hide errors in the final output without checking why they occurred.

<a id="intermediate-scenarios"></a>
### Scenario-Based Questions - Intermediate

#### Q1. You are supporting a UK retail chain and must prepare a monthly performance pack for finance and operations. Several teams disagree about the numbers, and Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS is central. What should you do? [Scenario]

- **A.** Delete outliers or missing values before checking what they represent.
- **B.** Agree definitions, profile the source data, build a repeatable solution using Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS, validate against source totals, and document caveats.
- **C.** Change the visual styling and avoid challenging the metric definition.
- **D.** Use a manual workaround that cannot be repeated next month.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of Joins: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q2. A refreshed deliverable for a London fintech team looks wrong while you compare regional performance where teams use different definitions. The investigation involves Subqueries. What is the strongest approach? [Scenario]

- **A.** Change the visual styling and avoid challenging the metric definition.
- **B.** Use a manual workaround that cannot be repeated next month.
- **C.** Trace the data grain, test the transformation or calculation path, use Subqueries deliberately, and review the result with the data owner.
- **D.** Bypass governance or access controls because the deadline is tight.

**Correct answer:** C

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of Subqueries, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q3. Your manager at an NHS reporting team asks you to combine multiple extracts before a senior stakeholder review with a repeatable process. How should you apply UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT? [Scenario]

- **A.** Use a manual workaround that cannot be repeated next month.
- **B.** Bypass governance or access controls because the deadline is tight.
- **C.** Optimise the final screen before checking source data grain or joins.
- **D.** Create a reproducible workflow, check edge cases, validate the output, and make the assumptions visible to stakeholders.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q4. You inherit a partially built query, view, stored procedure, or schema for a regional transport operator while trying to investigate why a refreshed dashboard shows unexpected totals. The weak area is String and date functions. What is the best response? [Scenario]

- **A.** Compare the current result with a trusted baseline, isolate where String and date functions affects the numbers, and fix the logic transparently.
- **B.** Bypass governance or access controls because the deadline is tight.
- **C.** Optimise the final screen before checking source data grain or joins.
- **D.** Assume another team has already validated the extract and skip reconciliation.

**Correct answer:** D

**Explanation:** This workplace scenario requires a controlled use of CASE statements, plus definition checks, validation, and clear communication. That is the standard expected in UK analyst roles around GBP 45,000+.

#### Q1. Which statement best explains the role of Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, PARTITION BY in SQL for UK data analytics work? [Concept]

- **A.** It improves reliability, interpretability, and auditability before decisions are made.
- **B.** It is mainly a visual styling choice with no effect on analytical quality.
- **C.** It guarantees that all source data is correct even when no validation is performed.
- **D.** It removes the need to agree metric definitions with stakeholders.

**Correct answer:** A

**Explanation:** Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, PARTITION BY matters because it improves the quality, traceability, and business meaning of the query, view, stored procedure, or schema. Analysts at this level are expected to validate and explain their work.

#### Q2. Which practical step best demonstrates correct use of Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, PARTITION BY in a query, view, stored procedure, or schema? [Tool-Usage]

- **B.** Use the tool feature deliberately, check a small sample, and document any assumption in the query, view, stored procedure, or schema.
- **C.** Change every field to text to avoid thinking about data types or aggregation.
- **D.** Delete records that do not match the expected conclusion before investigating them.

**Correct answer:** B

**Explanation:** Correct use of Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, PARTITION BY means applying the right SQL capability and checking it against row counts, join cardinality, execution plans, constraints, and sample records. That keeps the analysis repeatable and credible.
